intro_script: Welcome to Robotics Daily, I'm your host. On this Wednesday, July 21st, 2026, we've got six exciting updates for you. From a game-changer in LiDAR mapping to brain-controlled robot tech and four new robots from AGIBOT, it's shaping up to be an interesting day.
outro_script: "Alright, gearheads! That's all for today's robotic rebellion - may the code be with you! Thanks for joining me on this wild ride into the world of robotics. Until tomorrow, keep on coding and stay SLAMmin'!"

items.title: ROS Discourse General: OnSLAM: Run LiDAR-inertial mapping on ROS1 bags directly from Windows
items.summary: OnSLAM is an open-source Windows application that runs LiDAR-inertial odometry and mapping on ROS1 bag files. It automatically detects compatible sensors, visualizes the trajectory and point-cloud map in a browser, and exports PLY and PCD files for easy inspection of datasets. The project aims to simplify the process of inspecting datasets without requiring a complete Linux and ROS environment setup. Users are encouraged to test the application with different sensors and provide feedback, particularly on Livox, Ouster, Velodyne, or Hesai data.
items.audio_script: From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: OnSLAM: Run LiDAR-inertial mapping on ROS1 bags directly from Windows". The open-source application OnSLAM has successfully integrated into the AI/tech landscape, enabling users to run LiDAR-inertial odometry and mapping on ROS1 bag files directly from a Windows environment. This innovative solution simplifies the process of inspecting datasets without requiring a complete Linux and ROS setup. By utilizing OnSLAM, users can automatically detect compatible sensors, visualize their trajectory and point-cloud map in a browser, and export PLY and PCD files for easy dataset inspection. The project's creators are now encouraging users to test the application with various sensors, particularly those from Livox, Ouster, Velodyne, or Hesai, to further refine its capabilities and gather valuable feedback.

items.title: BrainCo demonstrates brain-controlled robot AI platform
items.summary: BrainCo has developed a brain-controlled robot AI platform that allows users to direct a robot through neural signals in under 200 milliseconds. The technology works by decoding brain signals and converting them into commands for the robot, which can perform tasks such as grasping objects with precision. BrainCo's Embodied AI Data Collection Solution addresses the lack of high-quality training data for robots, capturing EEG data from human operators to record both physical actions and brain intent.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "BrainCo demonstrates brain-controlled robot AI platform." BrainCo has unveiled a groundbreaking brain-controlled robot AI platform that enables users to direct robots through neural signals in under 200 milliseconds. This innovation leverages EEG data from human operators to capture both physical actions and brain intent, bridging the gap between physical and cognitive interfaces. The technology allows robots to perform tasks with precision, such as grasping objects. BrainCo's Embodied AI Data Collection Solution addresses a critical need for high-quality training data in robotics by converting neural signals into actionable commands. This development promises to revolutionize the way humans interact with robots.

items.title: AGIBOT unveils four new robots at WAIC 2026 as it expands industrial embodied AI portfolio
items.summary: AGIBOT unveiled four new robots at WAIC 2026, expanding its portfolio of humanoid robots, industrial automation systems and dexterous robotic hands. The launches include the AGIBOT A3 Ultra humanoid robot, X2 EDU education platform, G2 Max industrial robot and OmniHand 3 Ultra-M dexterous robotic hand.
items.audio_script:
From Robotics & Automation News, "AGIBOT unveils four new robots at WAIC 2026 as it expands industrial embodied AI portfolio." AGIBOT has announced the launch of four new robotic products at the World Automation Convention (WAIC) 2026, significantly expanding its portfolio of humanoid robots and industrial automation systems. The new additions include the AGIBOT A3 Ultra humanoid robot, which features advanced artificial intelligence and machine learning capabilities, as well as a dexterous robotic hand called OmniHand 3 Ultra-M. Additionally, AGIBOT unveiled the X2 EDU education platform, designed to provide interactive and immersive learning experiences for students, and the G2 Max industrial robot, optimized for precision and efficiency in manufacturing processes. These new launches are expected to further solidify AGIBOT's position as a leader in the development of embodied AI solutions.

The launch of these new robots is part of AGIBOT's ongoing efforts to integrate artificial intelligence into its product portfolio, enabling users to create more intelligent and autonomous industrial systems. By expanding its offerings, AGIBOT aims to meet the growing demand for advanced robotic solutions in industries such as manufacturing, education, and healthcare. The company's strategic expansion plans are expected to drive innovation in the AI and robotics sector, with significant implications for businesses and organizations looking to adopt cutting-edge technologies.

The introduction of these new robots marks an exciting development in the world of embodied AI, where machines are designed to interact with their environment in a more human-like manner. With its expanding portfolio, AGIBOT is poised to play a key role in shaping the future of robotics and automation, driving innovation and adoption across various industries.

items.title: Building robots for unpredictable, infrastructure-free environments
items.summary: Robots that can operate effectively in unpredictable, infrastructure-free environments face significant challenges, including reliability, environmental variability, and the need for real-world deployment to accumulate data. Most robotics companies fail because they optimize for controlled lab conditions rather than real-world scenarios. Burro Robotics, which operates in outdoor agricultural settings, has learned about tolerance and environmental variability through hands-on experience, and believes that a fundamentally different engineering achievement is needed to perform reliably across all conditions. Infrastructure-free localization and perception are key areas of research investment that will unlock the next phase of autonomous robotics for industrial environments.
items.audio_script:
From The Robot Report, "Building robots for unpredictable, infrastructure-free environments," a new phase of autonomous robotics is emerging as companies like Burro Robotics adapt to real-world conditions. Robots designed for controlled lab settings are often unable to function reliably in outdoor agricultural environments, highlighting the need for engineers to focus on tolerance and environmental variability.

Burro Robotics has learned about these challenges through hands-on experience in its operations, leading to a new approach that prioritizes infrastructure-free localization and perception. By focusing on developing robots capable of adapting to diverse conditions, companies can unlock significant advancements in autonomous robotics for industrial environments. Research investment in this area is expected to yield substantial benefits.

The success of Burro Robotics suggests that the next phase of autonomous robotics will prioritize environmental variability and real-world deployment, leading to more reliable and effective robots in challenging outdoor settings.

items.title: ROS Discourse General: PyZeROS: a Python-first alternative to `rclpy` over Zenoh
items.summary: PyZeROS is an experimental Python-first alternative to `rclpy` for communicating with ROS 2, offering minimal dependencies, no ROS installation required, and asyncio executor. It can install through pip and communicates with ROS 2 through Zenoh, interoperating with standard ROS 2 nodes using rmw_zenoh_cpp. PyZeROS is designed for asyncio and asynchronous Python, providing custom ROS messages defined directly with Python classes.
items.audio_script:
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: PyZeROS: a Python-first alternative to `rclpy` over Zenoh." PyZeROS is an experimental Python-first alternative to `rclpy`, offering minimal dependencies and no ROS installation required. It can install through pip and communicates with ROS 2 through Zenoh, interoperating with standard ROS 2 nodes using rmw_zenoh_cpp. PyZeROS is designed for asyncio and asynchronous Python, providing custom ROS messages defined directly with Python classes. This new alternative was made possible by leveraging the capabilities of asyncio executor and Zenoh to create a seamless integration between ROS 2 and Python-based systems.

PyZeROS achieves this through a combination of asynchronous programming and direct integration with ROS 2's rmw_zenoh_cpp library, allowing users to define custom messages directly in Python classes. This approach eliminates the need for traditional ROS message definitions, making it an attractive option for developers seeking to streamline their workflows. The development team has effectively utilized pip installation and asyncio executor capabilities to create a lightweight yet feature-rich solution.

The emergence of PyZeROS is significant, as it offers a more accessible entry point for Python developers into the world of ROS 2 programming. With its focus on minimal dependencies and ease of use, PyZeROS has the potential to bridge the gap between the Python and ROS communities, facilitating greater collaboration and innovation in the field of robotics and AI.

items.title: Integrate NVIDIA Omniverse RTX Sensor Simulation Into Existing Apps
items.summary: NVIDIA Omniverse libraries provide modular APIs including ovrtx for RTX-based sensor simulation (camera, lidar, radar) from OpenUSD scenes, supporting real-time, physically grounded outputs for integration into existing application stacks. The lightweight C and Python SDK enables applications to configure render and sensor outputs, step simulation from the app loop, and retrieve output tensors on CPU or GPU.
items.audio_script:
From NVIDIA Developer Blog, "Integrate NVIDIA Omniverse RTX Sensor Simulation Into Existing Apps." This story is about NVIDIA's latest development in integrating its Omniverse libraries, which allows developers to seamlessly integrate real-time, physically grounded sensor simulations from OpenUSD scenes into existing application stacks.

NVIDIA's omniverse libraries provide modular APIs, including ovrtx, that enable developers to simulate RTX-based sensor outputs such as cameras, lidar, and radar. This is achieved through the use of lightweight C and Python SDKs, which allow for configuration of render and sensor outputs, step simulation from the app loop, and retrieval of output tensors on CPU or GPU. By integrating these capabilities, developers can create more realistic and immersive experiences in their applications.

This integration is poised to significantly enhance the AI and tech industries by providing developers with a versatile toolset for simulating real-world sensor data, paving the way for further innovation in fields such as autonomous vehicles, robotics, and virtual reality.

items.title: Real-Time sEMG-Based Telecontrol of an Assistive Robotic Arm Using a 1D Convolutional Neural Network
items.summary: A real-time sEMG-based telecontrol system for an assistive robotic arm using a 1D convolutional neural network (CNN) achieves high classification performance, with a test accuracy above 90%, and stable real-time behavior. The system uses four-channel sEMG acquisition, signal preprocessing, segmentation into sliding windows, and classification. It is implemented in simulation and on a real robotic platform, producing coherent and smooth movements. The system demonstrates the feasibility of sEMG-based telecontrol for assistive robotics.

items.title: S.E.A.G.R: A Socially and Emotionally Aware Greeting Robot Framework with Dual-Layer Cultural and Affective Modulation
items.summary: S.E.A.G.R, a socially and emotionally aware greeting robot framework, has been developed with dual-layer cultural and affective modulation. The system combines context-aware cultural mapping, emotion-based gesture modulation, and proxemic regulation in a unified Sense-Think-Act architecture. It aims to provide adaptive greetings for humans from diverse cultural backgrounds and different emotional states, addressing the limitations of existing static greeting routines.

items.title: PRISM: Multimodal Terrain Mapping for Rover Navigation in Unstructured Environments
items.summary: PRISM, a multimodal perception system, leverages a custom sensor suite to capture RGB, depth, and thermal (RGB-D-T) imagery for terrain mapping in unstructured settings. At its core is OmniUnet, a novel vision transformer-based network designed for multimodal semantic terrain segmentation. PRISM efficiently generates traversability maps that enable autonomous navigation via a rover's Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) subsystem.

items.title: Linear Stability Analysis of an INDI Pitch-Rate Controller under Model Mismatch for a Tilt-Rotor VTOL UAV
items.summary: A closed-form fifth-order transfer function is derived for the full controller-estimator-actuator-plant interconnection, and stability is characterized through the Routh-Hurwitz criterion over a parameterized linear model. Two representative three-parameter sweeps produce interpretable stability regions, and two uncertainty-aware tuning procedures are proposed: robustness-oriented design that maximizes gain margin and phase margin, and performance-oriented design that maximizes worst-case closed-loop bandwidth subject to margin constraints.

items.title: Certifiable Safe Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Control-Affine Dynamics Approximation
items.summary: This paper proposes a safe reinforcement learning framework that learns control-affine dynamics with a certifiable data-driven safe policy using control barrier functions (CBF). It uses Control-Affine Random Fourier Features (ARFF) to model robot dynamics in a control-affine form and adaptive conformal prediction (ACP) to quantify the uncertainty in the safety constraint. The proposed framework demonstrates effectiveness on simulation results for the cartpole and 3D quadrotor platforms.

items.title: Foresight Residual RL for Long-Horizon Robot Manipulation with Vision-Language-Action Models
items.summary: Foresight Residual RL for Long-Horizon Robot Manipulation with Vision-Language-Action Models optimizes handoff quality by augmenting subtask rewards with an offline-estimated foresight value, the probability of future subtask success conditioned on the terminal state of the current subtask. The method achieves 85.6% full-task success in a three-phase wrench-based nut-tightening assembly task, outperforming standard subtask residual RL and VLA baselines, while leaving per-subtask success unchanged.

items.title: Differentiable Reinforcement Learning for Path Tracking by an Agile Fish-Like Robot
items.summary: Prashanth Chivkula and his co-authors developed a simulation platform that approximates the motion of a fish-like robot with computational efficiency. They applied PID control, where gains were learned using back propagation through time and training on a curriculum, to perform motion control and path tracking by the robot. The policy learned in the simulation was then applied on the physical platform, demonstrating an excellent match for agile fish-like robots.
